sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, after a demonstration of, of humanity, they locked our doors in the boxcars.
And we were on our way, away from Szegholom.
They took us to a place called Szolnok-- S-Z-O-L-N-O-K-- which was a larger city.
And there was a, a transient concentration camp set up.
Our gendarmerie from Szegholom left us.
And there were new soldiers and, and policemen surrounded there.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were Jews brought there from several areas, the different towns and different areas.
I think we were thousands there.
I couldn't tell you exactly how many.
We kept outside.
We were sleeping outside.
We had to put our blankets down.
And everybody was like herrings, lying down on the ground and sleeping there and eating whatever food and sandwiches we brought, some of them from my English teacher, of course.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The facilities were non-existent.
You couldn't wash.
You couldn't [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] go to the toilet, except for a large latrine which was dug there and prepared for us.
It was a huge, long latrine with the-- a wooden beam in the middle.
And you must excuse me [LAUGHS] for the topic.
But for a child who comes from a, a very neat, middle-class home, and suddenly falls in, into this hell, everything had a special significance.
If I may say, when I was a child, I would never go to the bathroom anywhere but at home in my house.
Even if I was at friend's house, I would run home to have it done at home.
Because I was kind of nervous [INAUDIBLE] kid.
And here we are.
There is that latrine where women, men, children were sitting on that wooden beam next to each other to do their things.
And some people fell in, into the-- into the feces.
It, it was a horror.
Nobody hid anything on.
There was minimal jewelry in, in my house, for instance.
There was never any particular point made of acquiring the things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was horrible.
And there was our-- there was our district doctor from my little town, Dr.
Neusbaum, who was a fine, upright citizen with a great heart who always took care of, of the whole population for free.
And he would always refuse any-- any payment, even.
His wife would take a duck from a peasant secretly.
And he would candy in one pocket and give it to the children in the street and, and bread crumbs for the dogs on the street.
He looked like George Bernard Shaw.
And he was a wonderful, wonderful old man.
And this was the first person I saw on the, on the ground while the policemen were kicking him in the head and the body.
Where did you hide the money?
This, this man didn't have anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These were the shocks that were building upon me, one after the other.
Were these policemen who were doing this Germans or Hungarians?
Hungarian.
They were Hungarians.
There were a few Germans that we could see then, few SS.
But they were still not running the show.
That was still done by the Hungarian army and police and gendarmerie.
Well, it was horror.
I, I just couldn't imagine that, that, that exists, that anything like this.
I, I lived such a sheltered life in my childhood with loving parents, the neatness that you have to take off your shoes when you go home and, and wash your hand, and, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and care.
And here suddenly, you fall in this hellhole.
And the fear-- a child knows at age 14 what life and death is.
What-- I didn't know what suffering is yet.
But fear, I already knew.
And we read about the Germans.
We heard about Germans.
There were rumors, what they do to the Jews.
And, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was one of the most fearful time that, that I spend at the beginning.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, I would ask them, what do you think what's going to happen to us?
Oh, and they reassured me.
Don't worry.
We're all together.
And, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we will see.
If we have to work, we will go to.
Work and we will work hard.
And we will survive.
My parents were very positive.
And, and my mother was very well-organized and that she kept us together.
They didn't have the answer, of course.
They couldn't tell me anything what happened or what will happen, mainly.
But I, I shared my fears with them and with my sister who, who hovered above me and around me always and protected me, because she was five years older.
She came home.
I jumped back again.
She lived in, in a different part of the country, where she was a optician [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the Western part of Hungary.
And she had to come home when things became tighter.
And we knew that the Germans came in.
So my parents sent a, a helper from my house, a Christian, to go and fetch her home.
So this guy put a big cross on her neck when they were on the train.
And that's how he brought her home, my sister, Eva, so she was not molested or she was not taken away.
She did not wear the yellow star at, at that period.
So she helped me a lot.
How?
Well, she was always tender and loving and, and shared her food with me.
And, and just by being together with this-- this family base gave me strength to go through with anything.
And that was true for the future, too.
Yes.
In this camp.
I might tell you that everybody was numb.
Everybody felt like somebody hit you with a, with a ton, with a jackhammer.
And we could hardly dare talking and discussing the situation and what will happen.
Because these people were all around us all the time and were taking-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] grabbing people to take them away to question and to torture and to beat up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I can't recall any specific conversation on what the future would bring, because it, it was a total darkness.
We didn't know.
It was a, an expectation, a state of expectation, I might say.
The Jews?
They were not surprised, no.
Nobody was surprised I was actually surprised there weren't more people there.
There was just a limited number of people.
It just meant that the majority of, of the village was not really cheering.
Several days-- it, it was a, a short period.
It looked and felt like eternity, first because it was not organized, because there were no facilities.
Even in the camp when you are in a camp, in, in a German death camp, there is some organization.
[LAUGHS] They kill you in an organized fashion.
But this, it was just an ad hoc transient camp with, with horrible place.
And besides, that was the first.
Later on, as you get the kicks, you kind of get used to it more.
You're, you're more-- you're more apt to accept what's happening to you.
But this was the first.
And to me as a child, that, that was the-- really the worst experience, the beginning of our tragedy.
So we were there just a few days.
And one day, the Germans came.
And they demanded from the president of the [NON-ENGLISH] of the community, that he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] should make two lists, split the community into two.
He should make two lists of people in an equal number, which he did.
My family was put in one list.
Some friends were put in the other list.
My father's brother and his family was put in the other list.
There's nothing we could do about it.
We didn't know why and how and what, what the reason was for that.
So one morning early, the Germans came and the gendarmerie again.
Everybody stood up.
Get your packages.
And they separated the two groups according to the list.
They took us again to the railroad station.
And here came the boxcar again.
The journey in the cattle car, they put us, about 80 of us, in, in one cattle car.
I don't know anybody has an-- any idea what is it to be in a box car with 80 other people.
And the other group, group from the list went to another train.
Nobody had any idea where.
Pardon?
Well, it's a, it's a, a large train with cattle cars.
Everybody saw a cattle car.
And when it passes by and you see the movies with the hobos sitting in the cattle car, one or two with their legs dangling in the door.
And they're playing banjo.
But that was not the case.
You put 80 people there with their little packages and then close the door.
And they lock it.
And you have a little window with the barbed wires.
And that's all the light and air comes in-- no food, no drinks, no destination.
You don't know where you're going.
You don't know where the train will take you.
And this train, somehow to me, even today, the row and the train of the cattle cars symbolizes to me our fate with, with no end in sight, with no knowledge what will happen to you, just fear.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the train would start.
And the engine begin to pull.
And you go.
And you don't know where you're going.
And just by judging the direction, somebody said, I think we're going toward Germany.
And that, even to me as a child, it's somebody grabbed my heart.
That's fear of Germany.
The name Germany meant possible death.
It, it took us a, a few days to go eventually to Austria.
And while we--
Yeah.
Well, it was summer.
It was June, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the end of June or maybe the beginning of July.
But it was very hot, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people next to each other like in a can of sardines.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You're hungry.
You're thirsty.
Mainly, you're thirsty.
There's nothing to drink.
So as we pass by the fields, the train stops here and there.
And people are working on the field.
And we were hollering.
The adults were hollering through that little opening, water, water.
Please give us some water.
I felt like Quasimodo when he's asking for water.
So that peasant would look up with his shovel in his hand.
He said, hey, Jew?
Do you have a gold watch?
Throw me a gold watch, I give you some water.
That's a picture I still see very sharply if, if I had taken a photograph.
So the train went by.
And it went ahead.
And eventually, we, we arrived to Austria, as I learned later.
It was another camp.
It was called Strasshof.
The train stopped a screeching halt.
And they evacuated us before [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] a German SS came from boxcar to boxcar with a bowl and said, you give me all your valuables.
Otherwise, you'll be shot.
So everybody gave.
Nobody took any risk to keep anything, not even a, not even a ring.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I told you before that I would trade my sandwiches for my stamp collection.
I took out my stamp, stamp collection.
And I threw it in his bowl.
To me, that was more than a, a, a diamond ring or, or gold necklace or anything else.
To me, that was my jewelry, my valuable, the most valuable.
And even years after, I was thinking of this stamp collection.
And, and I really never took, took on again.
So we arrived to Strasshof.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they disinfected us.
You had to undress and go through all sorts of examinations, naked men and naked women.
And doctors checked us out.
And we stayed there I think two days or three days.
It was an organized camp, in order to distribute people from these different areas.
And from this camp, we were sent in a train to an agricultural area in Austria.
It was called a village that was called [INAUDIBLE].
We were received by the mayor of the town with a swastika who, later on, turned out to be quite a decent fellow.
Somehow, I was lucky.
We were lucky.
We always met some decent people among the, the beasts.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They put us in a house.
It was a small group of people, maybe 30 or 40 of us in that little village, all Hungarian Jews from different areas.
And we had to work on the, on the fields, digging ditches or picking potatoes or in the fields and whatever work occurred in, in those fields.
And my father was named the head of this group.
And he was the, the trustee.
The village people, as I understood, were better in Austria than in Vienna.
They were less Nazis.
And they were less convinced that they will win the war.
There were one farmer who had one arm.
And we asked him, what happened to your arm?
This was a rather young man.
He said he lost his arm in the Soviet Union.
And I lost this for Hitler, he says.
And I hope the war will end very soon.
And he took us to his house.
And he put the radio on.
Ta-ta-ta-tum-- the BBC, he was listening to it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the other hand, the mayor's son was a little Nazi who was my age.
And, and he called me a dirty Jew.
So it depended what education they got.
These, these kids were all in the Hitlerjugend.
The work in the fields and probably that few months that we spent there-- and we ate relatively well-- help us to survive the war.
Eventually, we went, went to a concentration camp.
That was from the end of June or from the beginning of July until late October.
While we were working on the fields, somebody whispered to us that there was an assassination against Hitler, which unfortunately failed.
We learned that.
We also learned, I think from the one-armed man, that the Allied landed in Normandy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we learned it later, of course.
Because when this happened, we were still in, in the ghetto.
And my memories from this period [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were a mixture of, of relative well-being, being outdoors on the fields and working.
And when you know when you work, you know that they're not going to kill you.
Maybe we are useful.
Maybe they need us.
Or maybe, maybe it was reassuring to, to have a few people like, like the one-armed Austrian and the mayor with the swastika who was quite decent.
So as fall came, the work ended.
And they gathered us.
And we said goodbye to the farmers.
Then again, they took us to railroad station.
And here, we go back to my old friend, the cattle car, the boxcar.
And again, in, in different stations, there more and more people were put in the wagon, so several thousands of people were collected again in a very organized German fashion.
And away we go, several days again.
The train was going, as far as I remember, north, day after day, slowing down and going faster.
And again, you have no idea where you're going.
It became already colder.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that time, we-- the Germans threw in a, a few pieces of cheese and some water.
So we didn't starve to death yet.
And we still didn't know why there were two lists in Szolnok in Hungary, why a community was separated.
If I am allowed to mention it--
At this point, that we learned after the war, that the second list went to Auschwitz and perished.
The first list were us.
Why?
Why were we spared?
Why did we live?
There was a Jewish-Hungarian person whose name was Kastner who had a deal with the Germans.
And Mr.
Kastner said that he would get money and trucks and equipment for the German army in order to trade with the Jews.
So this agreement came about.
And just as they agreed and signed contracts and so on and so forth, the Germans needed trucks and gasoline.
And they needed everything.
And this man promised that he could get it for the Germans.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that list consisted of people that would be possible material for Mr.
Kastner to exchange with the Germans.
We didn't know it then yet.
But we learned it later.
So actually, Mr Kastner saved our lives.
He saved our lives.
And unfortunately, later on, he was assassinated in-- in his room by some mad person who, who felt that his family was not saved.
Or Mr.
Kastner had, had favoritism, which was not true.
Because Mr.
Kastner never met my family.
We had nothing to do with him.
I just want to mention it in memory of Mr.
Kastner.
Oberstein [INAUDIBLE].
[SIGHS] So the weather became colder.
There was fog.
And there was rain.
And we arrived to a railroad station that bore the sign Bergen-- B-E-R-G-E-N.
It meant nothing to us.
We got out of the cattle cars.
And from that railroad station, they marched us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hundreds of people, marched us to a destination which we didn't know, of course, what it was.
And eventually, we arrived to an area which was fenced with barbed wires and gates and barbed wires and towers, guard towers.
And that was the first time I had ever encountered, ever met, ever seen a, an extermination camp.
Yeah.
